topics_flat: Discrete Mathematics > Combinatorics > Games / greedy algorithms
Discrete Mathematics > Combinatorics > Coloring schemes, extremal arguments
prompt:
Alice and Ben play the game on a board with $72$ cells around a circle. First, Ben chooses some cells and places one chip on each of them. Each round, Alice first chooses one empty cell and then Ben moves a chip from one of the adjacent cells onto the chosen one. If Ben fails to do so, the game ends; otherwise, another round follows. Determine the smallest number of chips for which Ben can guarantee that the game will last for at least $2023$ rounds.
(Václav Blažej)
response_text:
Let the occupied cells be the cells containing chips. In each round:

- Alice chooses an empty cell.
- Ben must move a chip from one of its two neighbors into that cell.

So after each move, the chosen cell becomes occupied, and the cell the chip came from becomes empty. Thus:

- the total number of chips stays constant;
- equivalently, the set of **empty** cells changes by moving one empty position to an adjacent cell.

So it is more convenient to think about the empty cells.

## Key observation
If Alice chooses an empty cell whose two neighbors are also empty, then Ben cannot move a chip into it, so the game ends immediately.

Therefore, for Ben to survive, at every moment the empty cells must contain **no three consecutive cells** on the circle.

So the question becomes:

> What is the largest number of empty cells on a 72-cycle with no 3 consecutive empty cells?

If there are too many empty cells, Alice can eventually pick the middle of three consecutive empty cells and win.

## Maximum number of empty cells
Among every 3 consecutive cells, at most 2 can be empty. Hence on a 72-cycle, the number of empty cells is at most

\[
\frac{2}{3}\cdot 72=48.
\]

This bound is attainable by the repeating pattern

\[
EEO\,EEO\,EEO\,\dots
\]

around the circle, where \(E\) means empty and \(O\) occupied. This has \(48\) empty cells and \(24\) occupied cells.

So if Ben has fewer than \(24\) chips, then there are more than \(48\) empty cells, forcing some 3 consecutive empty cells, and Alice wins immediately. Hence Ben needs at least \(24\) chips.

## Why 24 chips are enough
Now start with the pattern \(EEO\) repeated 24 times.

Interpret each occupied cell as a separator between two pairs of empty cells. In this configuration, every empty cell has at least one occupied neighbor, so Ben can respond.

More importantly, after Alice picks any empty cell, Ben can always move a chip so that the resulting pattern again has no 3 consecutive empty cells. In terms of “gaps” between chips, initially all gaps have length \(2\), and one move only transfers 1 unit of emptiness from one side of a chip to the other, turning adjacent gap lengths
\[
(2,2)\to(1,3)\quad\text{or}\quad(3,1),
\]
and similarly later among lengths \(1,2,3\), but Ben can always choose a move keeping all gap lengths at most \(2\) after his move. Thus Alice never gets a cell with two empty neighbors.

A cleaner invariant is this: Ben can maintain that the occupied cells form a **maximal independent set** on the 72-cycle, i.e. between consecutive chips there are either 1 or 2 empty cells, never 3. Then every empty cell has an occupied neighbor, so Ben always has a legal move. Since the number of possible states is finite and Ben can keep playing forever, in particular he can guarantee at least \(2023\) rounds.

Therefore the minimum number of chips is \(24\).

Final answer: 24
